    We have already seen in warmup: locally small family fibrations and globally small categories that smallness in the sense of the definition above appropriately generalizes the ordinary notion of smallness for categories over \SET . Another perspective on smallness is given by the internal language, in which a category is viewed as an algebra for the “theory of categories” computed in another category with enough structure. The notion of internal categories is credited (independently) to Alexander Grothendieck and Charles Ehresmann.

    The notion of a (meta-)category is an essentially algebraic theory. As such it is possible to compute models of this theory in any category that has finite limits.

    Definition (internal category) #frct-001A

    Let E be a category with finite limits; then an internal category in E is defined by the following data:

    1. an object of objects C\Sub{0}\in E ,
    2. an object of morphisms C\Sub{1}\in E ,
    3. source and target morphisms s,t:C\Sub{1}\to C\Sub{0} ,
    4. a generic identity morphism C\Sub{0}\to C\Sub{1} ,
    5. a generic composition morphism C\Sub{1}\times\Sub{C\Sub{0}}C\Sub{1}\to C\Sub{1} ,
    6. satisfying a number of laws corresponding to those of a category.

    For the details of these laws, we refer to any standard source.

    The Externalization of an Internal Category #frct-000V

    Construction (externalization) #frct-001R

    Let C be an internal category in E . We may define a fibered category \Extrn{C} over E called the externalization of C .

    1. Given x\in E , an object of \Extrn{C}\Sub{x} is defined to be a morphism x\to C\Sub{0} in E .

    2. Given x,y\in E and f:x\to y and u \in \Extrn{C}\Sub{x} and v\in \Extrn{C}\Sub{y} , a morphism u\DispTo{f} v is defined to be a morphism h : x\to C\Sub{1} in E such that the following diagram commutes:

    Lemma (cartesian lifts in the externalization) #frct-000W

    The externalization is a cartesian fibration.

    Proof.

    Given an object v\in \Extrn{C}\Sub{y} and a morphism f:x\to y in E , we may define a cartesian lift \InvImg{f}v\DispTo{f} v by setting \InvImg{f}v = v\circ f : x \to C\Sub{0} .

    Lemma (globally small externalization) #frct-000X

    The externalization is globally small

    Proof.

    We may choose a generic object for \Extrn{C} , namely the identity element (C\Sub{0},\Idn{C\Sub{0}})\in \TotCat{\Extrn{C}} . Given any object (x,u)\in \TotCat{\Extrn{C}} the cartesian map (x,u)\to (C\Sub{0},\Idn{C\Sub{0}}) is given as follows:

    Lemma (locally small externalization) #frct-000Y

    The externalization is locally small.

    Proof.

    Fix x\in E and u,v\in \Extrn{C}\Sub{x} , we must exhibit a terminal object to the (total) category \TotCat{\CandHom{\Extrn{C}\Sub{x}}{u}{v}} of “hom candidates” defined in the definition of local smallness. First we define \brk{u,v} to be the following pullback in E :

    We define \overline{p}:\InvImg{\brk{u,v}}{u}\DispTo{p} u\in \Extrn{C}\Sub{\brk{u,v}} to be the cartesian lift of u\in \Extrn{C}\Sub{x} along p:\brk{u,v}\to x :

    We need to define a displayed evaluation map \epsilon : \InvImg{\brk{u,v}}u\DispTo{p} v ; unraveling the definition of a displayed morphism in the externalization of C , we choose the following diagram:

    Putting all this together, we assert that the terminal object of \TotCat{\CandHom{\Extrn{C}\Sub{x}}{u}{v}} is the following span in \Extrn{C} :

    Fixing another such candidate hom span \brc{u \leftarrow \bar{h}\rightarrow v}\in\TotCat{\CandHom{\Extrn{C}\Sub{x}}{u}{v}} , according to the definition of local smallness we must exhibit a unique cartesian morphism \bar\alpha : \bar{h}\to \InvImg{\brk{u,v}}{u} making the following diagram commute:

    First we note that the evaluation map \epsilon\Sub{h} : \bar{h}\to v amounts to an internal morphism h\to C\Sub{1} satisfying the appropriate compatibility conditions. Therefore we may define the base \alpha:h\to \brk{u,v} of the universal map using the universal property of the pullback that defines \brk{u,v} :

    The morphism \alpha:h\to \brk{u,v} defined above is the unique map in E satisfying the conditions required of the base for \bar\alpha ; therefore, it suffices to show that there exists a cartesian morphism \bar\alpha:\bar{h}\DispTo{\alpha}\InvImg{\brk{u,v}}u since it will be unique if it exists. We define \bar\alpha using the universal property of the cartesian lift:

    That \bar{\alpha}:\bar{h}\DispTo{\alpha}\InvImg{\brk{u,v}}u is cartesian follows from the generalized pullback lemma for cartesian morphisms: it suffices to observe that both \bar{p}\Sub{h}:\bar{h}\to u and its second factor \bar{p}:\InvImg{\brk{u,v}}u\to u are cartesian.

    The Full Internal Subcategory Associated to a Displayed Object #frct-0011

    The full subfibration associated to a displayed object \bar{u} of a locally small cartesian fibration E over B can be seen to be equivalent to the externalization of an internal category \Intrn{\bar{u}} in B .

    Construction (the internal category associated to a displayed object) #frct-003Q

    Let \bar{u} be a displayed object in a locally small fibration E over B . We will define the internal category \Intrn{\bar{u}} in B associated to \bar{u} . In particular, we let the object of objects \Intrn{\bar{u}}\Sub{0} be u itself; defining the object of arrows \Intrn{\bar{u}}\Sub{1} is more complex, making critical use of the local smallness of E over B .

    We will think of the fiber category E\Sub{u\times u} as the category of objects indexed in the boundary (source and target) of a morphism. Restricting \bar{u} along the source and target projections, we obtain the displayed objects of “points of the source” and “points of the target” respectively:

    Because E is locally small, there is an object \Hom{E\Sub{u\times u}}{\bar\partial\Sub{1}}{\bar\partial\Sub{2}}\in \Sl{B}{u\times u} that behaves as the “generic hom set”. We define \Intrn{\bar{u}}\Sub{1}\in B and its source and target projections to be this very object.

    Theorem (characterization of the externalization) #frct-001S

    The externalization \Extrn{\Intrn{\bar{u}}} of the internal category \Intrn{\bar{u}} associated to a displayed object \bar{u} in a locally small fibered category E over B is equivalent to the full internal subfibration \FullSubfib{\bar{u}} .

    Proof.

    We will define a fibred equivalence F : \Extrn{\Intrn{\bar{u}}}\to \FullSubfib{\bar{u}} over B .

    1. Fix x\in B and \chi\Sub{x} \in \Extrn{\Intrn{\bar{u}}}\Sub{x} , i.e. \chi\Sub{x} : x\to u ; we define F\prn{\chi\Sub{x}} to be an arbitrary cartesian map \phi\Sub{x} : \bar{x}\DispTo{\chi\Sub{x}} \bar{u} . (Here we have used the axiom of choice for collections.)

    2. Fix f : x\to y\in B and \chi\Sub{x} :x\to u and \chi\Sub{y}:y\to u and a diagram representing a displayed morphism h from \chi\Sub{x} to \chi\Sub{y} over f as below:

      We must define F\prn{h}:\bar{x}\DispTo{f} \bar{y} , fixing arbitrary cartesian maps \bar\chi\Sub{x}:\bar{x}\DispTo{\chi\Sub{x}}\bar{u} and \bar\chi\Sub{y}:\bar{y}\DispTo{\chi\Sub{y}}\bar{u} . First we lift h:x\to \Extrn{\Intrn{\bar{u}}}\Sub{1} into E using the universal property of the cartesian lift:

      By composition with the “evaluation map” for our hom object, we have a map \bar{x}\DispTo{f;\chi\Sub{y}}\bar{u} :

      Next we define F\prn{h}:\bar{x}\DispTo{f}\bar{y} using the universal property of (another) cartesian lift:

    The Internalization of a Small Fibration #frct-000Z

    Let E be a small fibration over B a category with finite limits, i.e. a cartesian fibration that is both locally small and globally small. We will show that E is equivalent to the externalization \Extrn{C} of an internal category C in B , namely the full internal subcategory associated to the generic object \bar{u}\in E .

    Proof.

    By the characterization of the externalization we know that the externalization of C so-defined is equivalent to the full subfibration \FullSubfib{\bar{u}} of E spanned by objects that are “classified” by \bar{u} . Because \bar{u} is generic, we know that every object of E is classified by \bar{u} , so we are done.

    Other Kinds of Fibrations #frct-0012

    Cocartesian Fibrations #frct-0015

    Cocartesian fibrations are a dual notion to cartesian fibrations, in which the variance of indexing is reversed.

    Definition (cocartesian morphism) #frct-0016

    Let E be displayed over B , and let f:x\to y \in B ; a morphism \bar{f}:\bar{x}\DispTo{f} \bar{y} in E is called cocartesian over f when for any m:y\to u and \bar{h}:\bar{x}\DispTo{f;m} \bar{u} there exists a unique \bar{m} : \bar{y}\DispTo{m} \bar{u} with \bar{f};\bar{m} = \bar{h} :

    We use a “pushout corner” to indicate \bar{x}\to\bar{y} as a cocartesian morphism, a notation justified by the dual self-indexing.

    Definition (cocartesian fibration) #frct-0017

    A displayed category E over B is a cocartesian fibration when for each f : x \to y\in B and \bar{x}\in E\Sub{x} , there exists a displayed object \bar{y}\in E\Sub{y} and a cocartesian morphism \bar{f} : \bar{x}\DispTo{f} \bar{y} .

    Cocartesian fibrations are also known as opfibrations.

    The Total Opposite of a Displayed Category #frct-0018

    Construction (total opposite category) #frct-001I

    Let E be displayed over B ; we define its total opposite \TotOpCat{E} displayed over \OpCat{B} as follows:

    1. An object of \TotOpCat{E}\Sub{x} is given by an object of E\Sub{x} .

    2. Given f : x \to y\in \OpCat{B} , a displayed morphism \bar{x}\DispTo{f} \bar{y} in \TotOpCat{E} is given by a displayed morphism \bar{y}\DispTo{f} \bar{x} in E .

    Warning. Do not confuse this construction with the opposite fibered category, which produces a displayed category over B and not \OpCat{B} .

    Exercise #frct-001J
    Let E be displayed over B . Prove that the total category \TotCat{\TotOpCat{E}} is \OpCat{\prn{\TotCat{E}}} , and its projection functor is \OpCat{\prn{p\Sub{E}}} : \OpCat{\TotCat{E}}\to\OpCat{B} .
    Exercise #frct-001K
    Let E be displayed over B , and let f:x\to y\in B . Prove that a morphism \bar{f}:\bar{x}\DispTo{f}\bar{y} is cartesian over f in E if and only if \bar{f}:\bar{y}\DispTo{f}\bar{x} is cocartesian over f in \TotOpCat{E} .
    Exercise #frct-001L
    Prove that a displayed category E is a cartesian fibration over B if and only if \TotOpCat{E} is a cocartesian fibration over \OpCat{B} .

    Example (revisiting the fundamental self-indexing) #frct-002X

    Recall that the fundamental self-indexing \SelfIx{B} of a category B is a displayed category with \SelfIx{B}\Sub{x} = \Sl{B}{x} . As discussed in #frct-001Y, \SelfIx{B} is a cartesian fibration over B if and only if B has pullbacks. However, \SelfIx{B} is unconditionally a cocartesian fibration.

    Exercise #frct-002Y
    Prove that \SelfIx{B} from the fundamental self-indexing is a cocartesian fibration for any category B .

    Example (the dual self-indexing) #frct-0019

    Dually to the fundamental self-indexing, every category B can also be displayed over itself via its coslices \CoSl{x}{B} .

    Construction #frct-002Z

    Let B be a category. Define the displayed category \overline{B} over B as follows:

    1. For x\in B , define \overline{B}\Sub{x} as the collection of pairs (\bar{x}\in B,p\Sub{x}:x\to\bar{x}) .
    2. For f : x\to y\in B , define \overline{B}\Sub{f} to be the collection of commuting squares in the following configuration:
    Exercise #frct-001M
    Prove that \overline{B} is a cocartesian fibration if and only if B has pushouts.
    Exercise #frct-001N
    Prove that the total category of \overline{B} is the arrow category B\Sup{\to} , and the projection is the domain functor.
    Exercise #frct-0030
    Prove that \overline{B} is a cartesian fibration for any category B .

    Right Fibrations #frct-0013

    Definition (right fibration) #frct-001O
    A cartesian fibration E over B is said to be a right fibration when all displayed morphisms in E are cartesian.
    Remark #frct-003P
    Recall from fiber categories and vertical maps that for every b\in B , the collection of displayed objects E\Sub{b} and vertical maps E\Sub{1\Sub{b}} forms a category. When E is a right fibration over B , this category is in fact a groupoid.
    Theorem (characterization of right fibrations) #frct-001P

    A cartesian fibration E over B is a right fibration if and only if all its vertical maps are isomorphisms.

    Proof.

    Suppose that E is a right fibration over B , and fix b\in B , \bar{b}\in E\Sub{b} , and a vertical map f:\bar{b}\DispTo{1\Sub{b}} \bar{b} . Using the hypothesis that f is cartesian, it has a unique section g:\bar{b}\DispTo{1\Sub{b}} \bar{b} as follows:

    Likewise, because g is cartesian, f is the unique section of g ; thus f is an isomorphism in E\Sub{b} .

    Conversely, suppose that E is a cartesian fibration whose vertical maps are isomorphisms. Fix f:x\to y \in B and an arbitrary displayed morphism \bar{g}:\bar{x}\DispTo{f}\bar{y} . Then \bar{g} is the precomposition of a cartesian lift \bar{f}:\bar{x}\tick\DispTo{f}\bar{y} with a vertical map:

    Because vertical maps are isomorphisms and \bar{f} is cartesian, we can observe that \bar{g} is cartesian as follows, writing \bar{m} : \bar{u}\DispTo{m} \bar{x}\tick for the unique factorization of \bar{h} through \bar{f} over m :
